blocks.body: Negative questions are questions with a negative form that usually express surprise, surprise, or the expectation of a positive answer. They can sound like criticism or sarcasm, so it is important to understand the context of their use.

{ "title": "10. Brief synopsis", "items": [ "Negative questions — questions with a negative form of the auxiliary verb.", "Structure: Don't/Doesn't/Didn't + subject + verb / Isn't/Aren't + subject.", "Meaning: surprise, expectation of a positive response, polite request or criticism.", "With do/does/did: Don't you know? / Doesn't she work here? / Didn't they come?", "With to be: Isn't she happy? / Aren't you ready? / Wasn't it good?", "With modals: Can't you swim? / Won't she come? Shouldn't we go? — Yes, I do (I love).", "Use the pattern in the examples.", "Use the pattern in the examples.", "No = agreement with the negative: Don't you like it? — No, I don't (I don't like).", "Context is important: intonation determines whether it is surprise, criticism or politeness." ] }
